header: Gerente de People & Culture — Maple Bear
hub_subtitle: Business English para People & Culture, comunicação executiva e a liderança da divisão do Canadá

lesson.menu_title: The First 90 Days -- Listening Before You Lead
lesson.menu_desc: Taking the reins of the Canadian division without arriving as a stranger who changes everything on day one: run a listening tour, report back what the outgoing director and the frontline staff actually said, and earn credibility before spending it -- entry plan, listening tour, ramp-up period, learning curve, credibility, mandate, predecessor, transition period, frontline staff, vantage point, handover, to hit the ground running, to take the reins, to size up, to lean on. Structure: reported speech + reporting verbs (she explained that the team had been drifting / he urged me to slow down / they warned me not to restructure / the staff admitted that morale was low). -- 36 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 12 -- The First 90 Days
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Danielle Moreira | Lesson 12 -- The First 90 Days
phases: The Language of the First 90 Days

listenings.text: Danielle, it is Gordon, recording this before I hand you the keys on Monday. I ran this division for six years, so let me give you the one thing the handover document will not. When I arrived, I was so sure of what needed fixing that I restructured the academic team in my first month, and I was wrong about almost all of it, because I had changed the answer before I understood the question. So here is what I would do differently if I were you. Do not decide anything for ninety days. Spend that time on a listening tour instead. Sit with the frontline staff, the people who run the classrooms, not just the directors, and ask them what is actually broken and what you must not touch. They told me things in year three that would have saved me year one, and the only reason they told me late is that I did not ask early. You have a real mandate here, and the temptation will be to spend it fast to prove you deserve it. Resist that. Credibility in this office is not given with the title. It is earned in the first quarter, one honest conversation at a time, and once you have it, everything else gets easier.

Danielle, it is Ruth, your coach. You asked me how to handle the first ninety days without either freezing or overreaching, so here is the short version. The ramp-up period is not wasted time, even though it will feel like it, because you are used to operating at full speed and suddenly you cannot. That discomfort is the learning curve, not a sign that you are failing. The mistake almost every senior hire makes is to treat the transition period as an audition, so they change something visible in week two just to look decisive. Do the opposite. Build a simple entry plan with three phases, learn, decide, act, and do not let anyone push you out of phase one early. When a stakeholder challenges you and asks why you have not fixed anything yet, that is not a threat, it is your cue to say, plainly, that you are still listening on purpose. And lean on Gordon. He made the fast mistakes so you do not have to, and a predecessor who is honest about what went wrong is the most useful person you have in the first quarter. Size up the culture before you try to move it.

reading.rtitle: The Field Note She Wrote on Day 89
paras: Every senior leader who inherits a team feels the same pull in the first week: to arrive with the answer. You were hired because you are good at fixing things, so the fastest way to prove you belong is to fix something visible, immediately. Danielle's predecessor, Gordon, admitted that he had done exactly that -- he told her he had restructured the academic team in his first month and had been wrong about almost all of it, because he had changed the answer before he understood the question. His advice was the opposite of his instinct: decide nothing for ninety days, and spend the ramp-up period on a listening tour instead.

So she did. Over eleven weeks she sat with the frontline staff, not just the directors -- the people who actually run the classrooms -- and she asked the same two questions in every conversation: what is genuinely broken, and what must I not touch? The answers surprised her. One coordinator explained that the last three reorganizations had each been announced as urgent and then quietly reversed, so the team had learned to wait out any new plan rather than commit to it. Another admitted that morale was low, but insisted the cause was not the workload; it was that nobody in head office had ever asked them anything before deciding. A regional director warned her not to mistake their politeness for agreement, and pointed out that a Canadian team will rarely tell you no to your face.

None of this was in the handover document, and none of it could have been. It came from a particular vantage point -- the classroom floor -- that a new leader only reaches by asking early and listening long. The temptation the whole time was to act, because a mandate feels like something you are supposed to spend, and every week she did not restructure anything felt like a week of wasted authority. But credibility, she was learning, is not spent with the title. It is earned in the first quarter, one honest conversation at a time, and the surest way to lose it on day one is to change something before the people living with it believe you understand it.

By day eighty-nine she had a plan, and it was smaller and sharper than the one she would have written on day one. She had learned to lean on Gordon, whose early mistakes were now her shortcuts, and she had learned to size up a culture before trying to move it. The entry plan she finally presented had three phases -- learn, decide, act -- and she was still, deliberately, in the first. When a stakeholder asked why she had not fixed anything yet, she no longer heard a threat. She heard her cue to say, plainly and without apology, that she was still listening, on purpose, and that the listening was the work.
reading.source: Field note, People & Culture -- Danielle's first quarter, Maple Bear Canada

gist.prompt: Read once for the main idea. Which sentence is the argument of the whole text?
choices: A new senior leader should restructure the team quickly, in the first month, to prove she deserves the role.
Because credibility is earned, not granted by the title, a new leader should spend the first ninety days listening -- especially to frontline staff -- before changing anything.
Handover documents contain everything a new leader needs, so the listening tour is an optional courtesy.
Canadian teams never disagree with a new leader, which makes the first ninety days easy to manage.

items: Gordon told Danielle that restructuring the academic team in his first month had been the right move.
The opposite: he admitted he had been wrong about almost all of it, because he had changed the answer before he understood the question.
Danielle held her listening tour with the frontline staff, not only with the directors.
She sat with the people who actually run the classrooms and asked what was broken and what she must not touch.
One coordinator explained that past reorganizations had been announced as urgent and then quietly reversed.
That is why the team had learned to wait out any new plan rather than commit to it.
The regional director warned her to take the team's politeness as clear agreement.
He warned her NOT to mistake their politeness for agreement -- a Canadian team will rarely tell you no to your face.
The text says credibility comes automatically with the job title.
It says the opposite: credibility is not spent with the title; it is earned in the first quarter, one honest conversation at a time.

items.situation: "You have been here six weeks and changed nothing. When do you actually start leading?"
tips: I am leading -- I am on a listening tour by design, not by hesitation. I told the team I would decide nothing for ninety days, and keeping that promise is itself the first decision.
reframe the inaction as a deliberate phase, and report the commitment you made out loud

items.situation: "Just tell me what the frontline staff said. Give me the headline."
tips: One coordinator explained that past reorganizations had all been reversed, so the team waits out new plans; another admitted morale was low, but insisted the cause was never being asked, not the workload.
use reported speech -- explained that, admitted that, insisted that -- to relay it precisely, without inventing a verdict

items.situation: "Gordon left a mess. Why would you lean on the person who caused it?"
tips: Because he was honest about it. He warned me not to repeat his fast mistakes, and a predecessor who tells you where he was wrong is the most useful person you have in a transition.
defend the value of an honest predecessor; his errors are now your shortcuts

items.situation: "The Canadian team seems perfectly happy. So there is nothing to fix?"
tips: A regional director warned me not to mistake their politeness for agreement -- he pointed out that they will rarely say no to my face. So I am reading the room from their vantage point, not mine.
name the high-context signal, and hedge the read rather than overclaiming harmony

items.situation: "Your mandate is huge. Why not use it now, while you have it?"
tips: Because a mandate spent before I understand the question buys the wrong change. Credibility is earned in the first quarter, one conversation at a time, and once I have it the same mandate goes further.
distinguish spending authority from earning credibility; delay is an investment, not waste

items.situation: "Summarize your first ninety days in one sentence for the board."
tips: I ran a listening tour before I restructured anything, so the plan I am bringing you is smaller, sharper, and built on what the frontline staff actually told me -- not on what I assumed on day one.
the close: contrast the day-one plan with the day-89 plan, and credit the listening
